title: Chapter Eighteen: Orsino and Marco
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D13+D5 | Flashback: Venice, 1743 -->
*The Fluidity of Us — Apex Shaw*

The lavender cannot hide the sickness. I have arranged sprigs of it on every surface — the nightstand, the windowsill, the mantle above the cold fireplace — and still the room smells of what it is: a place where a man is dying. The lavender is sharp and green and alive, and beneath it, patient as the tide, the sour-sweet rot of consumption works its way into everything. The curtains. The pillows. My clothes. My throat.
I sit in the chair beside the bed and I watch Marco breathe.

He is propped against a mountain of pillows, each one embroidered by hands that were not mine but were paid for by my name. His skin has gone translucent. I can see the veins at his temples, blue rivers beneath ice. His cheekbones are so sharp they could cut the candlelight — and the candlelight is all there is, the winter afternoon outside the window a flat gray thing that offers nothing, takes everything. The candles gutter in their sconces and Marco's face moves between gold and shadow, gold and shadow, and I think: this is what it looks like when the world cannot decide whether to show him to me or take him away.
He is beautiful. He is so beautiful that I want to break something.

I am immortal. I carry a transformation inside me that will reshape my body however it needs to survive — that will bend my flesh around death the way water bends around a stone. But it is only my body. Only mine. The gift, the curse, the thing in my blood that will not let me go — it has no reach beyond my own skin. I can become anything and I cannot save him. The transformation is a door that opens only inward, a room with no exit but my own survival, as if the universe looked at me and decided that my continuity mattered more than my love.
The universe was wrong.

Marco's hand moves across the counterpane. It takes him effort — I can see the cost of it in the way his jaw tightens, the way his breath catches on a small hard sound — but he reaches for me. His fingers find mine.

His grip is weak. This is the thing I cannot bear: the weakness of it. Marco's hands were strong. He rowed me through the back canals at night when the water was black glass and the only light was the moon's reflection breaking apart beneath the oar. He held my face in both hands and kissed me like he was trying to press a secret into my mouth. Now his fingers rest in mine like a question he is too tired to finish asking.
"You look at me," he says, "like you're already mourning."
The taste of candle wax is in the air — tallow and smoke, the flat mineral flavor of heat. I swallow it down.
"I am always already mourning," I say. "I have been mourning you since the day I understood what you were to me."

Marco laughs. It is a wet, terrible sound, thick with the fluid building in his lungs, and I hate it and I love it because he is still laughing, because even now the absurdity of the world reaches him. Even now.
"You speak," he says, catching his breath, "like someone who has practiced grief."
"I have."
His eyes find mine. They are still Marco's eyes — dark, clever, tender, lit with something that the sickness cannot touch. "Tell me."
"Every time I look at you, I practice. Every time you fall asleep, I rehearse the hour after. I have lived the moment of losing you a thousand times. It does not help."
"Good," he says. "It shouldn't. If it helped, I would be furious."
I laugh. It comes out broken, but I laugh, because he is right. If the rehearsals had prepared me, if I had found some way to make this smaller, it would be an insult to what we have been.

"Orsino," he says. The way he says my name — it has always been a whole sentence. Tonight it means *come closer*. I lean forward. The chair legs scrape the tile floor and the sound is loud in the quiet room and I wish I had lifted the chair because that sound should not be the last thing he hears.
"I want you to write me a letter," he says.
I blink. "A letter."
"Something I can hold when I'm gone." His thumb moves across my knuckle — a flicker of the old tenderness, already fading. "Something that will survive me."

I stand. The parchment is on the writing desk by the window, and the sealing wax — red, the color of everything I am about to say — is in the drawer. I carry them back to the chair and I balance the page on my knee and I dip the pen and I stop.

Because what do you write to a dying man who has asked you for something that will outlast him? What words are large enough? What language will fit inside a parchment fold and still mean something when the hands that held it are ash?
I write.
*My beloved,*

*I have loved you in masks and in moonlight. I have loved you on the water and in the dark. When you are gone, I will wear your absence like a second skin. I will carry you in every form I take. You are the first. You are the reason I know what love is.*

*Yours until the stars go out,*
*Orsino*

I do not read it back. If I read it back, I will change it, and if I change it, I will make it smaller, and I will not make it smaller. I heat the wax over the candle flame — the taste of it sharpens in the air, tallow and resin and the acrid edge of smoke — and I let the red drop fall onto the fold. I press my seal into it. The ring on my own finger is plain gold, nothing like the emerald on his. My seal leaves a small device in the wax: a wave, because of course it is a wave, because I have always been the thing that changes shape while he was the thing that stayed.
I place the letter in Marco's hands.
He holds it to his chest. He presses it there, over his heart, or the place where his heart still beats, the place where the sickness has not yet reached. The parchment crinkles. His eyes close.
"Thank you," he whispers.
"Marco —"
"Don't." He opens his eyes. "Don't make it bigger than this. This is the right size. A letter. A sealed letter. It fits in my hands. That's enough."
I press my lips together. I taste wax and smoke.
"Will you love again?" he asks.

The question is so simple and so vast that I cannot answer it. I stare at him. The candlelight moves across his face. The emerald on his finger catches the flame and throws a green spark against the wall, a brief bright thing, there and gone.
"I don't know how to answer that," I say.

"Yes, you do." His voice is patient. He is patient the way only dying people can be — as if time has finally stopped being a threat and become a kindness. "Yes, you will. And when you do, I want you to remember something."
I wait.

"I was the first to have your heart. Not the last. The first." He smiles. The smile is real. It reaches his eyes, crinkling the skin at the corners, and for one moment — one long, luminous moment — he looks like the man who rowed me through the black canals, who kissed me against the wall of the Palazzo Mocenigo, who once said *I would follow you into any shape you took* and meant it. "That's the honorable position, Orsino. First. You can't take that from me. Not even death takes that."

He is smiling. He is dying. He is making a joke about his own death because he is extraordinary and because the alternative is screaming, and I know this, I know it in my marrow, because the alternative is what I am doing inside: screaming, screaming, screaming.

I press my forehead to his hand. His skin is cool. It should not be cool. Marco runs warm, has always run warm, and the coolness of his fingers against my brow is a trespass, a wrongness, a violation of the order of things. The emerald ring is right there, inches from my face, and the green of it is the only color in the room that means anything. I have always loved that ring. He wore it the night we met. He will wear it tonight. It is his.
"I will carry you," I say into his hand. "I will carry you in every form."
"I know," he says. "That's what the letter says. I held it. I know."
Later.
Much later.

The room is quiet. Not the quiet of candles and breath but the quiet of something finished. The candles have burned down to pools of wax in their sconces, and the gray light has given way to a darker gray, and the Grand Canal outside the window carries the sound of a gondola passing — one, then silence — and the silence is the loudest thing I have ever heard.
The breathing has stopped.

I sit on the edge of the bed. I am holding his hand. It is cold now — truly cold, not the cool of illness but the cold of a body that has begun its long departure from the world of the living. The emerald ring is loose on his finger. Loose because the flesh has already begun to thin, to pull away from the bone, to surrender the architecture that held it in place. He was so thin. In the end, he was so thin, and I could not put my arms around him without feeling the edges of him, the blunt truth of his frame, the skeleton arriving early to announce itself.
I do not weep.

I have been alive long enough — I have been alive too long and not long enough — to know that the first grief is the one that teaches you weeping isn't enough. The first grief is the one that breaks the faucet and finds the well dry. I will weep later. I will weep in the canal at night when the water is black and there is no one to see. I will weep in a hundred rooms, in a hundred bodies, across centuries that Marco will never touch. But here, now, in this room that smells of lavender and death and tallow smoke, I do not weep. I do something harder.
I reach for his hand. The left one. The one with the ring.

I hold his fingers — already stiffening, already becoming a thing rather than an extension of a person — and I slide the emerald ring from his finger. It comes away easily. Too easily. A ring should resist. A ring should cling. This one slips off like it was waiting for me, like Marco in his last moments had already loosened it, had already planned for this, had already arranged for me to take it because he knew — he always knew — what I would need.

I place it on my own finger. The band is warm from his skin. No — the metal is cold. It has been cold for some time. It will warm. That is what metal does. It takes the heat of the living and it keeps it for a while, and then it gives it to the night, and then it is cold again, and the only thing that makes it warm once more is another living hand.
My hand.

I look at the ring on my finger. The emerald is dark, almost black in this light, but I know its color the way I know the color of my own blood. It is green. It has always been green. Green as the water of the Grand Canal on a summer afternoon. Green as the moss on the stones of the Rialto. Green as the thing in me that will not die.

I stand. I cross to the writing desk. The parchment is still there, the spare sheets, and I take one and I write a single line on it — not a letter, not a confession, just a fact, just truth rendered in ink:
THE BRICKS OF THIS ROOM HOLD MY HEART. ORSINO, 1743.
I fold it. I seal it with the same red wax, the same wave device. And then I go to the wall.

The wall behind the bed — behind where Marco lies, behind where he held the letter to his chest — is plaster over brick, old, the mortar soft in places where the damp of the canal has worked its way through the stone. I find a place where the plaster has already cracked, where a brick is slightly proud of its neighbors. I work it free with my fingers. The plaster crumbles. The brick comes away in my hands — heavy, damp, cold — and behind it is a cavity, a gap between the inner and outer walls where the builders left a pocket of air that has held nothing but silence for two hundred years.
Until now.

I place the letter inside. I fit the brick back. I press the plaster into the cracks with my thumbs, smoothing it, sealing it, making the wall whole again. Anyone who looks will see only an old wall, a place where the plaster has been repaired. No one will think to look for what is behind it.
No one will think to look for three hundred years.

And then — and this is the part that matters, this is the part I need the future to understand — it will be found. Not by a stranger. Not by a workman with a chisel and no idea what he has uncovered. By someone with the same hands. By someone who was always meant to find it. By someone who will hold the parchment and read the words of a young man who was not yet ancient, who had not yet worn a thousand faces, who had only just discovered that love and loss are the same animal wearing different masks.
The letter is in the wall.
The ring is on my finger.

And somewhere — not here, not now, not yet — a man with Marco's cheekbones is standing in this same palazzo, and the water of the Grand Canal is still carrying gondolas, and the bricks are still holding their secrets, and the secret is this:
I have never stopped loving him.
I have simply continued the long, long work of finding him again.
